product.purpose: Convert an intent-to-buy into a completed, paid order with the least friction and the highest trust, then confirm it.
product.regulatory_sensitivity: moderate-to-high (payment data under PCI-DSS; consumer/PII and consent under GDPR/CCPA-class law)

users.frequency: infrequent, often a first or one-off checkout
goals: buy the chosen item quickly and feel safe paying
not be forced to create an account or re-enter known data
understand the total cost (taxes, shipping) before committing
recover gracefully from a card error or a flaky connection
check out in as few taps as possible with saved details

workflows: Guest checkout: enter shipping, choose delivery, pay, confirm, all without an account.
Express checkout: pay with a stored wallet (Apple Pay / Google Pay / saved card) in one or two taps.
Recover from payment failure: re-attempt or switch payment method without losing cart or entered data.
Apply a discount or adjust quantity: see the total update and re-confirm intent.
Resume an interrupted checkout: return after a dropped connection or app switch and continue where left off.

information.density: low (one decision per step; progressive disclosure; minimise on-screen choices)

environment.primary_device: mobile phone (touch, small screen, one-handed, frequently interrupted)
environment.connectivity: consumer mobile networks; low connectivity tolerance required, must survive flaky/slow links

risks: Conversion risk: any added field, surprise cost, or extra step measurably drops completion.
Connectivity loss between submit and confirmation creates fear of a double charge or a lost order.
Hidden costs (tax/shipping revealed late) cause abandonment and distrust.
Forced account creation pushes one-off shoppers away.
Payment errors with vague messaging strand the shopper instead of guiding a retry.
Mishandled payment/PII data breaches PCI-DSS and privacy obligations.
constraints: Mobile-first, thumb-reachable, large touch targets; avoid keyboard-heavy steps.
Each step must be idempotent and resumable so a retry never double-charges.
Total cost must be transparent before the final commit.
Guest checkout must be a first-class path, not a buried fallback.
Payment capture must be PCI-DSS compliant; PII collection must be minimal and consented.
verified: The primary user is a shopper completing a purchase.
Use is infrequent and the flow is conversion-critical.
Low connectivity tolerance is a stated requirement.
inferred: Guest and express (wallet) checkout matter most, inferred from infrequent, conversion-critical mobile use.
Idempotent submit/confirm is required, inferred from low connectivity tolerance.
Cost transparency before commit reduces abandonment, inferred from conversion criticality.
assumptions: Payment is handled via a third-party processor, so PCI scope is largely delegated.
A cart/catalogue system of record exists upstream.
US English with Spanish is a reasonable initial localisation set.
Wallet payment methods (Apple/Google Pay) are available to the audience.
unresolved: Actual baseline conversion and abandonment data to target improvements against.
Which payment methods and currencies must be supported per market.
Whether one-page or multi-step checkout performs better for this catalogue.
Real localisation footprint (currencies, languages, tax jurisdictions).
Accessibility commitments required by the operating markets.
evidence: task-brief: shopper, infrequent, conversion-critical, mobile-primary, low connectivity tolerance
inference-only: e-commerce conversion priors applied; no analytics, A/B data, or store access available
